behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Feature map: wiring=yes, real_infra=yes, model_judgment=no, multi_step_agentic=no. Recursive watch behavior depends on real filesystem event propagation and watcher backend behavior across directory depth, so real infrastructure is the minimum reliable level.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temporary watched root, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Feature map: wiring=yes, real_infra=no, model_judgment=no, multi_step_agentic=no. This is primarily coordination between event ingestion, dedup cache/windowing, and pipeline trigger wiring; no real OS behavior is strictly required to catch core failures.
behaviors.test_description: Inject two processing requests for the same canonical file path within the dedup window and assert only one pipeline execution occurs; then send a third request after the window and assert it executes.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Feature map: wiring=yes, real_infra=yes, model_judgment=no, multi_step_agentic=no. Duplicate low-level file events are OS/watcher implementation behavior, so real infrastructure is needed to expose and validate this failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: With a real watcher and filesystem, perform a write pattern known to emit multiple events (e.g., temp-write then rename/append), capture daemon processing count, and assert one output for the target file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Feature map: wiring=yes, real_infra=yes, model_judgment=no, multi_step_agentic=no. Directory deletion/recreation watch recovery is driven by OS notification semantics and watcher re-subscription behavior that mocks often miss.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real temp watch path, delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Feature map: wiring=yes, real_infra=yes, model_judgment=no, multi_step_agentic=no. Ordering by filesystem creation timestamps interacts with real metadata precision and event timing, so system-level validation is the safest minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files in rapid succession with controlled timestamp differences on a real filesystem, trigger ingestion, and assert processing order matches creation-time ordering rule.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Feature map: wiring=yes, real_infra=yes, model_judgment=no, multi_step_agentic=no. Partial-write detection depends on real write timing, file locking/flush behavior, and watcher event timing at OS level.
behaviors.test_description: Begin a slow/streamed write to a file in the watched directory, verify daemon does not process during active write, then close the writer and assert processing occurs once with full final content.
